segment-text: "IF I HAD A HAMMER"
ACT ONE:

FRONT TITLES: The front title sequence is over a STOCK FOOTAGE montage of the big news events of 1958 through early 1964 set to The Weavers' version of --If I Had a Hammer||: Elvis Presley, wearing an army uniform, waves as he boards an airplane for Germany; U.S. troops are shipped to Vietnam where fighting has begun in earnest; Chuck Berry is indicted for tax evasion; Little Richard quits music to become an ordained Seventh Day Adventist minister; Martin Luther King delivers his --I have a dream|| speech to a Washington, D.C. civil rights rally; students hold a sit-in at a university; John and Jackie Kennedy sit in the back-seat of the presidential LincolnContinental cruising down the streets of Dallas; Jack Ruby shoots Lee Harvey Oswald; LBJ is sworn in as president; the Beatles arrive in America . . . DISSOLVE:
A title reads: --Saturday, February 8th, 1964.|| EXT. --STUDENTS FOR POLITICAL ACTION|| OFFICE - DAY

The --Students For Political Action|| office is a small storefront located in a downtown area. Poor, inner-city people fill the streets. A 21-year old beatnik boy named MAX, with a goatee and a beret, steps up to the front door, pulls out a key, goes to insert it into the lock and the door swings open. Max finds this somewhat surprising and enters.
INT. --STUDENTS FOR POLITICAL ACTION|| OFFICE - DAY

Max steps into the ratty interior of the office--old and broken office furniture, torn easy chairs with stuffing hanging out, a torn, yellowing poster on the wall announcing The Weavers reunion concert at Carnagie Hall--and he finds the air full of cigarette smoke, which he waves away from his face. Max turns on the light. In the very back of the office is LORRAINE DEMPSEY, an attractive, intense 19-year old girl with a short, boyish haircut, wearing a plaid skirt and a sweater. She cranks a mimeograph machine which on each revolution spits out a yellow flyer stating in big bold letters, --Free the Springfield Five!! Emergency Meeting Sunday at 8:00 P.M.|| Lorraine sees Max and blinks, a smoldering cigarette butt dangling from her lips.
Lorraine, you're still here? That's exactly where I left you last night.
Lorraine squints through the smoke wafting up into her eyes.
I'm ready, Max. Nothing can stop me now.
You need to go to sleep.
(waves her hand) Nah! I slept all the time when I was young. (points) I've made 500 flyers, you think that's enough?
I should think so.
And you'll help me hand them out?
That's why I'm here.
Good. Y'know, Max, as I've stood here all night cranking this machine, it came to me that we're standing at a crossroads.
A crossroads to what?
To a new age.
That's what they were all saying about Kennedy and Camelot and all that, but look what happened.
It's bigger than that.
Yeah, I think you've been up too long.
Hopefully, it will be a time when stuff like this . . . (waves flyers) . . . won't happen anymore. I mean, if we don't do something right away these five boys will spend the
rest of their young lives in jail.
Hey, you don't have to convince me. But their pre-trial hearing is Monday. If they go in there with some cracker, redneck, court-appointed attorney, they'll get life for sure.

I know! This could well be their last chance. That's why it's so important and that's why I'm so nervous. This is the first important political action that I've personally organized. People just have to show up.
I'll be there.
I mean, real people?
Gosh, thanks a lot. (Lorraine smiles) Look, it's an important cause, why wouldn't they show up?
(thinks) Um . . . to make me look bad?
Make you look bad to whom, if I may ask?
Myself.
Is that what this is all about, Lorraine? Looking good?
No, not at all. It's entirely about those five boys and the injustice that's being done to them. I mean, if I don't do something about it why should I expect anyone else to do anything, right?
Right.
We all have to draw a line somewhere and say, --If you go beyond that line you have to deal with me, too.|| At least, that's what I think.
Right. Me, too. You could always just tackle people out in the street and drag _em in kicking and screaming.
I might yet.
I bet you would, too. But why do you do this, Lorraine, staying up all night, burning it at both ends?
Someone's got to, right?
Do they? Why?
Because if nobody cares the whole world will go to hell.
But, Lorraine, you can't save the whole world.
No, but I can try. And step one is getting everyone to care about this issue, the Springfield Five.
But you can't actually make anyone else care about something, Lorraine. Not really.
Oh, sure you can. These are old, time-worn techniques used by the wobblies before the war. But getting to a whole group at once, that's the trick. That's where songs come in.
(skeptical) Yeah, but even songs won't make someone care if they don't.
If it's the right song being sung the right way.
I don't think that's true.

(off-handed) Sure it is. Deep down I honestly think that people really want to do what's right and actually do care, they just need a little push in the right direction to get them going, and that's where I come in.
Lorraine reaches into her sweater pocket and pulls out a pack of cigarettes, which she finds empty and crumples.
Gotta smoke?
No. You're an idealist, Lorraine.
I'm more than idealist, Max, I'm an instrument of God.
Lorraine checks out the overflowing ashtray and spots a long butt. She straightens it out and lights it, then takes a sip of coffee. She winces and sticks out her tongue.
Blah! How can coffee get colder than the room it's in?
I don't know. You going to the Purple Onion tonight?
Oh yeah, sure. Got to. (she waves a pile of flyers) I have to get every single person at the Purple Onion tonight to come to my meeting Sunday. Those people are what I have to work with; they're
materials and this meeting is my testing ground. If
I have to yell and scream, I'll do it. If I have to whisper and sing songs, I'll do that.
And you dig it.
Yeah, yeah, that, too. (snaps her fingers) That reminds me, have you got an extra guitar pick?
Not here. You can always use a matchbook.
They shred. That's OK, I'll swing by the music store.
Lorraine sits down and puts on her shoes. Max checks out her legs as she does this. Suddenly . . .
Lorraine, you drive me crazy! Why won't you go out with me?
Look, Max, you're my friend. Let's leave it at that, OK?
(frustrated) But I don't want to leave it at that.
If there was something here, Max, we'd know about it by now, right?
I know about it.
(shrugs) It takes two to tango.
But what can I do, Lorraine?
Lorraine smiles and hands Max a thick pile of flyers.
Here. Hand these out. See ya later, alligator.
Lorraine walks out of the office and Max watches her go, groaning audibly after she's left.
EXT. --STUDENTS FOR POLITICAL ACTION|| OFFICE - DAY
Lorraine exits the office and strides up the street immediately attempting to hand out flyers.
Demand justice now! Free the Springfield Five! Most people take the flyers that are offered to them, although many immediately
discard them, some right on the street. When Lorraine sees this she goes over and picks the flyer back up.
(to herself) Slob!
EXT. THE BUCKLEY HOUSE - DAY

This is a small house in a neighborhood of nearly identical homes. Big leafy trees line the shady street. A red 1960 Ford Falcon sits in the driveway. The distinctive sound of the Three Stooges playing on TV can be heard from within. A car pulls up in front of the house.
INT. THE BUCKLEY HOUSE/LIVING ROOM - DAY

DAN BUCKLEY, a 14-year old wearing a Boy Scout uniform, sits watching the Three Stooges on TV and laughing. The doorbell rings. Dan dashes over and opens the door. Two other 14-year old boys in Boy Scout uniforms come bursting in and they all begin talking at once. Dan and his pals leave. MR. BUCKLEY and MRS. BUCKLEY enter the living room. Phil's Dad is wearing a bowling shirt and holds a bowling ball up in front of his face.
Be careful with that in the house.
Hon, please, I've got to get in the proper frame of mind, OK? (chuckles) Get it, --frame|| of mind? Bowling. Frame
That's great, dear, you're another Red Skelton.
(imitates Red Skelton's lisp) Good night and God blesh. (looks around) Where's Phil?
(shakes her head) Still in bed.
Mr. Buckley looks at his watch and frowns.
It's after ten, what's with him?
You tell me. Ever since he graduated high school and started work, he's changed. He just doesn't
seem to give a damn about anything anymore. I
don't know what to do with him. Mr. Buckley considers this for a moment, then exits the living room.
INT. HALLWAY OUTSIDE PHIL'S ROOM - DAY
Phil's Dad steps up to a closed bedroom door, tries the doorknob and finds it locked.
Phil? You up?
Yeah.
Aren't you supposed to be at work?
I got the day off.
How come?
It just happens that way sometimes.
Huh. Well, why don't you get out of bed.
Why? What's the difference?
Mrs. Buckley steps up beside her husband.
Because it's a beautiful day.
It's a beautiful day here in my bed, too.
I don't like this behavior, Phil.
Yeah?
Yeah. So get your ass out of bed!
Yeah, yeah, I will.
Do it soon.
All right. OK.
(shrugs) Y'know, Phil, if you waste this day, you'll never get it back.
Yeah? Big deal.
(to her husband) You see?
Yeah, I see.
What can we do?
How the hell am I supposed to know? (he kisses his wife) I gotta go. See ya.
Bye.
Phil's Dad and Mom exit and we stay on Phil's closed bedroom door.
INT. PHIL'S BEDROOM - DAY

PHIL BUCKLEY, a tall, handsome, eighteen-year old boy with a crewcut, lies in bed in his boxer shorts smoking a cigarette. Phil wears a can opener on a shoelace around his neck. The ashtray beside his bed is overflowing with smashed butts. Phil smokes languidly, the smoke drifting up from his nose. There are ashes on his chest, but he doesn't notice. Phil reaches under the bed and comes out with a can of beer. He opens it with his can opener, guzzles, winces, grins and belches. Phil glances over at the wall beside him. There are many photos of Elvis cut from magazines, as well as pictures of Tuesday Weld, Chuck Berry, Bobby Darin and Mickey Mantle. Phil suddenly sits up, reaches over and grabs an acoustic guitar that's leaning against the wall and begins strumming it. Phil plays a variety of tunes and rhythms in a half-assed fashion, but nothing sounds very good. He glances over at a book on his desk, --Beginning Guitar.|| Phil opens the book and begins to play. He's doing OK until he attempts to make a specific chord and, sadly, his fingers don't want to stretch that way.
Ouch!
Phil shuts the book. He walks over to his dresser, steps in front of the mirror, crosses his arms, lowers his chin and proceeds to do a poor Ed Sullivan imitation.
Tonight we have a really big shew. I mean, a really, really big shew. Let's all give a very
warm welcome to the talented, amazing, oneof-a-kind Phil Buckley!
to play my newest song which has been
number-one on the hit parade for over three months . . .

Phil sits down on the bed, strums his guitar, but still isn't getting the sound he wants. He thinks for a second, then puts down the guitar and digs through one of his dresser drawers. He comes up with a cheap old microphone with a strange, multi-pronged plug on the end. Phil considers this situation for a moment, then produces a Boy Scout knife and deftly cuts off the plug. He then splits the wire and shaves off the insulation. Phil takes the mike over to his record player and connects the bare wires to the back of the amplifier. Suddenly, feedback fills the room. Phil's eyes light up.
Boss!

He moves the mike away from the amp, then blows into it and hears his amplified breath through his speakers. Phil takes the microphone and shoves it into the hole of his guitar and presto! It's electric! Now he really starts to rock and it's clear that Phil is not particularly talented, simply enthusiastic. He does the Chuck Berry duck-walk across his room, guitar wailing.
INT. KITCHEN - DAY
Phil's Mom is doing the dishes. She hears the strange, electronic noise and looks all around.
What on Earth?
INT. PHIL'S BEDROOM - DAY

Phil's a rock & roll maniac playing to thousands of adoring fans and everything's terrific until - sproing! - one of his guitar strings breaks. Phil frowns, setting down his guitar. He scrutinizes the money on his dresser - he has one dollar to his name. Nevertheless, a guitar with a broken string seems like a fairly useless item. Phil stands up, finally motivated to do something. He puts his guitar in it's battered old case and begins putting on his clothes.
EXT. CITY STREETS - DAY

Phil walks along the street carrying his guitar case. Phil isn't paying any attention to anything going on around him. He's got a tune in his head and a vague, amused look on his face. He occasionally makes an Elvis Presley-style hip gyration accompanied by a grunt and we realize that Phil's still on The Ed Sullivan Show in his head. He adds the crowd reaction sounds, cheering and laughing. The beatnik kid MAX from the --Students for Political Action|| office offers Phil a flyer.
Free the Springfield Five! Let justice be done,
man. Meeting on Sunday at the Purple Onion.
Phil waves it away entirely uninterested.
No thanks.
INT. MUSIC STORE - DAY

This is the all-purpose variety of music store that sells most every sort of instrument and all the accessories. Lorraine stands before a large variety of guitar picks holding her guitar and deliberates. She chooses a pick, checks its thickness, then drops it back in its bin. Finally, Lorraine makes her decision, chooses a pick, then steps over to the counter and pays the portly, middle-aged CLERK a quarter. Lorraine strolls over to the sheet music. She flips through until she finds something she likes and pulls it out--it's Woody Guthrie's song --This Land is Your Land.|| Lorraine plays the first several chords, humming the tune. Something else catches her attention and she walks to another part of the store, stepping behind a big display case filled with woodwind instruments. Just then Phil steps up in front of the woodwind case, then crosses to the acoustic guitar section where Lorraine just stood. He finds the guitar string he needs and it costs fifty cents, half of his accumulated wealth. Phil resigns himself to this inevitability and replaces his broken string. He sees the portly Clerk watching him and smiles at the guy. The Clerk smiles back. Lorraine hands a yellow flyer to an OLD BLACK WOMAN, who puts on her glasses and looks at it with interest. Now Phil attempts to tune his guitar. He does a rather half-assed job, shrugs--that's good enough--then spots the open sheet music in front of him of "This Land is Your Land." Phil concentrates deeply, furrowing his brow, and painfully strums and sings the first few chords of the song.
(singing) This land is your land This land is my land From California To the New York Island From the redwood forests To the Gulf stream waters This land was made for you and me--
Lorraine steps up from behind and proclaims . . .
I love this song!
Phil turns around and checks Lorraine out. He smiles his coolest smile.
Really? No kidding? Me, too.
(excited) Really? Come on, let's play it together.
Lorraine begins playing the song and Phil very hesitantly follows along, glancing frequently at the lyrics.
(singing) As I went walking That ribbon of highway I saw above me That endless skyway I saw below me
This land was made for you and me

This land is your land This land is my land From California To the New York Island From the redwood forests To the Gulf stream waters This land was made for you and me I roamed and rambled And I followed my footsteps To the sparkling sands
While all around me A voice was sounding, sayin', This land was made for you and me The sun came shining And I was strolling
And the wheat fields waving And the dust clouds rolling As the fog was lifting A voice was chanting
Suddenly, everybody in the store, including the old black woman and the clerk, joins in for the finale.

This land is your land This land is my land From California To the New York Island From the redwood forests To the Gulf stream waters This land was made for you and me This land was made for you and me
Everybody laughs, then returns to their business. Lorraine turns to Phil.
Wow! Did you see the way the music draws people in? It's fantastic.
Yeah, it sure is. So, you like music, eh?
Yes, very much. Do you?
Oh, yeah. Uh, my name's Phil, what's yours?
(he puts out his hand to shake and she hands him a yellow flyer) There's a really important meeting tomorrow
night for the Springfield Five.
Phil takes the flyer looking confused.
Is that a new band?
No! Don't you read the newspaper?
Sure, but I must've missed it.
It's been the headline for the last week.
The neighbor's dog grabs our paper all the time
and chews it up. What's going on?
The Springfield Five are five colored boys that were arrested for no good reason as they were driving down south to a civil rights rally. We're trying to get them out of jail.
Oh. OK. Tomorrow night, huh?
(frowns) But tomorrow's Sunday, y'know.
Yeah? So?
So, Ed Sullivan's on Sunday at eight.
Lorraine grimaces with disdain.
Oh, that's too bad. I'm talking about real problems in the real world here.
I know, I'm just telling you that that's not a good time for a meeting.
Lorraine looks stricken.
You think? I printed 500 flyers.
(shrugs) It may not mean anything, y'know, maybe he
hasn't got anyone good on the show this week. Lorraine suddenly looks like she's got a headache. She reaches into her sweater pocket and finds it empty.
Have you got a cigarette?
Sure. Wanna get a cup of coffee to go with it?
Lorraine looks him up and down.
(grins) OK. Caffeine and nicotine are my favorite food groups.
Don't forget beer, it's just like liquid bread.
Lorraine laughs and she and Phil exit.
INT. COFFEE HOUSE - DAY
Phil and Lorraine both drink mugs of coffee and smoke cigarettes.
Well, I worked for a while at a crisis phone line,
but I got really tired sitting up all night listening to drug addicts moan about not scoring and pretending to be a marriage counselor. I didn't really feel like I was part of the fight.
(Phil nods, mesmerized) Right.
Lorraine really looks at Phil.
Do you know what I mean?
Sure.
Really?
(shrugs) No, not really.
Haven't you ever had a feeling of pure empathy?
(thinks, then shakes his head) I'm not sure. What's empathy?
It's like sympathy, only you don't feel bad for someone, you feel bad with them. (Phil digests this) Haven't you ever felt that way?
Oh, yeah. --Lassie|| does that to me almost every week.
(shakes her head) TV again! Jesus!
You don't like TV?
No, I don't. I think it makes people apathetic.
It's just entertainment.
And some entertainment has value. But mindless entertainment is useless. What are some of your interests?
(shrugs) Me? Oh, well, I have a wide range of interests.
(nods) Really? Like what?
Phil suddenly feels cornered.
Well, like everything.
(skeptical) Everything, huh? Interested in paleontology?
Do you know very much about paleontology?
(smiles) No.
(grins) I'm the foremost authority.
OK. So, what's your favorite subject?
You mean like in school?
No, I mean like in life.
Oh, that. Well . . . uh . . . music, I guess.
You sure don't sound convinced.
No, I am. Music. Definitely. I wanna be a musician.
(surprised) Really?
Yeah.
You know, it's really hard to be a musician. I take guitar lessons twice a week and that's not nearly enough.
(skeptical) Well . . . It all depends on what you're after, right?
(confused) What do you mean?
Well, that's if you want to be, say, a good or great musician.
(nods)
Right.
Well, Bobby Darin doesn't really have a great voice, but he's a very successful singer. Or what about Bob Dylan? He can't sing at all.
Yeah, but he's a great song writer.
Phil waves his hand in total deprecation.
You can hire guys to do that.
Yeah, so . . . ?
So, you don't necessarily have to be good to be famous. Look at Dean Martin.
Lorraine consider this for a second, then dismisses the whole thing.

That's silly. Of course you do. What are you talking about? If it's not their musical ability then it's their presentation. It's gotta be something. (Phil nods, good point; Lorraine remembers) Oh, y'know, tonight's Hootenanny night at the Purple Onion, you have to come.
(confused) Hootenanny? Is that like when you squaredance and stuff?
(laughs) No. Hootenanny night means that it's open microphone for anyone that wants to get up and sing or play or read a poem or do anything.
(nods) Will you be there?
(smiles and nods) Yes, I will. I'm going to sing a song tonight. You really should come, I mean, if you actually want to be a musician and all.
Right. I do. And I will.
Lorraine suddenly stands.
I've gotta go. So, will I see you tonight at the Purple Onion?
Sure. Absolutely.
(smiles) Great. Will you sing a song?
(shrugs) Do I have to?

No, you don't have to do anything you don't want to do. None of these people are professionals, it's just a hootenanny. But let's face it, Phil, if you can't sing at a hootenanny you'll never be famous as a musician.
(nods) Right.
(smiles) OK. See ya there if you're there.
I'll be there. Nice meeting you.
You, too.
Lorraine departs. The Waitress steps up and hands Phil the bill, which is 42 cents. He leaves his entire 49 cents.
(smooth) Keep the change, sweetheart.
The WAITRESS snorts and Phil leaves.
EXT. --STUDENTS FOR POLITICAL ACTION|| OFFICE - DAY

Lorraine stands in front of the office handing out yellow flyers and demanding Justice! A big, green 1963 Cadillac Sedan deVille with very pointy fins pulls up in front of her and stops. The car's electric window comes down revealing a good-looking couple in their early 40s. They are Lorraine's MOM and DAD.
Get in the car, dear, before someone mugs you. Lorraine sighs and gets in the back seat of the car.
(sarcastically) Thank God, saved at last.
INT. CADILLAC - DAY
Lorraine's Dad guns it and the car pulls away quickly. Dad glances back at Lorraine. DAD
Did you forget you were having lunch with us?
No, I didn't. I tried to, but I couldn't.
Her Mom looks back at her.
What have you got on your hands?
Lorraine looks at her hands and sees purple stains.
Ink.
(smiles) It's just your shade.
Yes, it is nice, isn't it?
So, did you get an early start on solving the world's problems today?
As a matter of fact, I did. I think I'll be having a very big meeting Sunday night.
I hope you do. But honestly, Lorraine, do you really think a bunch of college kids meeting in a low-rent downtown office are going to get those five Negro boys set free?
I certainly hope so. If nothing else it will raise people's awareness of the problem. A line has to be drawn somewhere.
Mom turns to Dad.
How did she turn out this way?
It's got to be your fault, I'm never home. MOM You can say that again.
Lorraine points at herself.
I'm my own fault. And where are we going for lunch, if I may ask? DAD The club.
(sighs) Unmitigated decadence, and the food's not very good.
You were such a happy little girl, Lorraine. What happened?
I developed a social conscience.
Well it certainly hasn't made you any happier, I can tell you that. Why don't you go away to Europe for a while - say a year - and make up your mind there?
I live here in America, mom. The problems of America are my problems. And my mind is made up.
Your mom's not kidding, Lorraine. You want to go to Europe for a year, see the sights, go to school, don't go to school. Anything you want, just say the word.
Oh, Dad, please.
You were accepted to the University of Florence, weren't you? You loved Italy when we all went there a few years ago. You ran around the house talking Italian for months.
(sighs wearily) This is such a bore.
Lorraine's Mom and Dad look at each other and shake their heads.
So, Lorraine, how many people are you expecting at this rally of yours?
I don't know. About a hundred, I'd say.
Well, how many chairs did you rent?
Fifty, but there's twenty or thirty chairs there and I figure some people can stand.
How're you getting the rented chairs there?
(exasperated) Dad, please, I'll work it out.
Look, you want to be an organizer and organizers bring in the people. If they don't bring in a decent crowd, you'll have to admit, they're not worth much as an organizer, right?
(begrudgingly)
Yeah, I suppose.

OK, how about this? If less than fifty people show up to your meeting, you consider doing something else. Anything else. More than fifty and I'll shut up, for a while. What do you say? But you have to give me a fair count.
All I did was agree to go to lunch, I'm not here to negotiate any deals.
Just think about it, OK? That's all I ask.
Whatever you say.
Lorraine sighs deeply, lost in the vast expanse of the back seat. Her hand goes into her sweater pocket, but she still doesn't have any cigarettes.
INT. PHIL'S BEDROOM - DAY
Phil sits on his bed noodling with his guitar. He has the newspaper on the floor and glances at it over the top of the guitar.
Folk songs, folk songs . . . Crap! I don't know any folk songs. (focuses on the newspaper) Hey! Lyndon Baines Johnson, Lady Bird Johnson,
Lynda Bird Johnson and Luci Baines Johnson?
Phil's Dad walks past the bedroom door, sees the newspaper and stops.
There's the newspaper. What's it doing in here?
I'm reading it.
Great day in the morning, what next? So, what's up?
Nothin'.
Y'know, I've been meaning to speak to you. (he comes in and sits down on the bed) So, Phil, what are you doing? You've been out of high school for six months.
Phil sighs.
I'm working at the shoe store. Knockin' _em dead, too.
What does that mean?
It means, Dad, that working at the shoe store is a bore.
You don't like it? Find another job.
Like what? Working in a gas station?
Well, don't you have some goal? Some ambition?
(guffaws) Yeah, I wanna be president and get my brains blown out.
(shocked) That's not nice.
It sure wasn't.
What's with you?
What?
Now come on, you must care about something?
(timidly) Well . . .
Yeah? Go on.
Well, I like playing the guitar.
(confused) You took lessons for about three weeks, then dropped out. How serious can you be about that?
(serious) Hey! People can be serious about things in their own ways, can't they?
(unconvinced) I suppose. Look, Phil, what about college? His Father lights a Viceroy cigarette.
Yeah, well . . . I'll probably go to community college in the fall.
I meant, a university. What about State? I went to State.
Phil points at his Dad's cigarettes.
Could I have cigarette?
His Dad shakes a cigarette out of the pack and gives him one.
You smoke too much.
So do you. I didn't get into State.
His Dad lights Phil's cigarette with a match, then his own.
If your grades are good at the community college you could get in.
Yeah? Well . . .
Phil shrugs and puffs on his cigarette.
You just don't care, do you?
Not about that.
So what do you care about?
Hey! Come on, Dad, lay off, will ya?
Fine. So what are you doing tonight?
I have a date.
His Dad grabs the wastebasket and uses it as an ashtray.
Really? Have I met her?
Uh-uh. I just met her today at the music store. She's a cute folkie girl that's really committed to all kinds of things.
(interested) Really? Like what?
(waves his hand) Oh, you know, Negroes and equality and things like that.
(impressed) Yeah, things like that. Well, she sounds interesting.
Yeah, she is. And cute, too.
Maybe you should become a folkie, then you'd be committed to all kinds of things, too.
I've been thinking about it.
The conversation grinds to an uncomfortable halt. They both sit there looking at each other. Finally, his Dad points at the guitar.
Go on, Elvis, play something.
Phil quickly considers for a moment, then hits upon a possibility.
Wait a minute.
What?
I think I've got it!
What?
What song to play.
Phil opens his beginning guitar book, finds what he's looking for and crudely begins to play and sing --If I Had a Hammer.||
(singing) If I had a hammer I'd hammer in the mornin' I'd hammer in the evenin' All over this land I'd hammer out danger I'd hammer out warnin' I'd hammer out love between My brothers and my--
Phil's Dad looks at his watch and jumps to his feet.
--Holy Christ! Look at the time, I gotta get dressed. His Dad abruptly exits.
--Sisters . . .
Phil sighs, picks up his cigarette, which has gone out, and puts it in his mouth. He takes his guitar over to the mirror and begins posing again.
The one, the only, Phil Buckley!
Phil strums the guitar, adds in the sound of the cheering crowd, and smokes his unlit cigarette like he's Frank Sinatra.
INT. PHIL'S HOUSE/ LIVING ROOM - NIGHT

Phil steps into the living room all dressed up and ready to go out, his guitar case in hand. His mother, father and brother all sit watching the black and white TV, while eating TV dinners at folding TV tables (Ronald Reagan is pitching --20 Mule Team Borax,|| saying, --I'm standing here in a borax mine . . .||). None of the three can look away from the TV for more than a brief second.
Where ya goin'
Out.
Where?
None o' your beeswax, ratfink.

Oh when the new world is revealed Oh when the new world is revealed Oh Lord I want to be in that number Oh when the new world is revealed Oh when the saints go marching in Oh when the saints go marching in Oh Lord I want to be in that number When the saints go marching in The Four Feathers bring down the house. The audience claps and stomps their feet. Lorraine, Terry and the whole table join them loudly. Phil honestly looks ill. He is in a total panic. His hands are sweating and he looks nauseous.
(to no one in particular) I think I gotta go.
Phil starts to stand and Lorraine reaches out and takes his arm.
What do you mean?
I can't do this.
Sure you can. You'll be fine.
(seriously) No, no, you don't understand.
(smiles reassuringly) Of course I do. Everyone feels this way their first time.
Yeah, but these people are really good.
Right. And their not professionals.
Yeah, but they really seem to . . . care.
Well, don't you?
(good question) Um . . .
Lorraine spots someone she knows and quickly exits.
_Scuse me.
Phil is in a deep quandary. He glances over at the door--he could just leave and no one would be the wiser. Meanwhile, Terry is watching him.
What's wrong?
(sighs deeply) Lorraine is doing the song that I was gonna do. I was all ready, too, y'know. I practiced the song like a hundred times. But now I'm screwed.
Terry takes pity on Phil.
Come with me. Everything'll be just fine.
What?
Just come on.
Terry leads Phil out the back door.
EXT. THE PURPLE ONION/ALLEY - NIGHT
Terry and Phil come out into the under-lit alley behind the Purple Onion. Terry pulls out a big, fat, crooked cigarette. Phil can't believe it. He looks all around to make sure they're alone.
(shocked) That's marijuana, isn't it?
Terry nods, lighting up.
Say it louder, maybe a cop'll hear you.
(frightened) But, it's illegal.
Yep.
Doesn't it lead to hard drugs like morphine and heroin?
(grins fiendishly) Whoa! Slow down, tiger. One step at a time.
Terry hands Phil the burning joint. Phil hesitantly takes it, gives the issue one more brief moment of consideration, shrugs, then takes a hit.
INT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT

Lorraine sits back down in her seat, looking somewhat embarrassed and straightens her hair. She notices that Phil and Terry's chairs are empty. She glances around as though people are looking at her and pulls her sweater closed. The M.C. steps up to the microphone, grinning, his hair askew.

Ladies and gentlemen, I'm proud to welcome back a Purple Onion regular, a dedicated fighter for equality and justice, and the organizer of the Springfield Five defense fund meeting here tomorrow at eight. Let's give a warm hand to
Lorraine Dempsey!
Lorraine throws a last look at the empty seats on either side of her where Phil and Terry used to be, shakes her head, smiles and heads up on stage holding her guitar.

Sometimes it's not easy to do what's right. We're always tempted to go the other way. To take the wrong path. And sometimes we don't even know which path we're on. Justice, freedom and equality are not just words. They're goals that we have to work for. That's why I strongly urge you to be here tomorrow night at eight P.M. for a very important meeting. We can make a difference . . .
EXT. THE PURPLE ONION/ALLEY - NIGHT
Phil and Terry are just finishing the joint. Their eyes are bloodshot and at half-mast. They're stoned.
(grinning)
Man, If I had a hammer I'd hurt myself.
They both laugh like total idiots.
So, what'dya think?
About what? (Terry waves his hand, Phil grins) Oh, yeah. Boss. Is it always like this?
Like what?
This?
Yeah.
Wow. How much is this stuff?
Pretty cheap.
And you got it from . . . um . . . (he can't remember) That Negro man.
Moustapha. Right.
Does he grow it.
No, man. He gets it from someone else who gets it from someone else who brings it from Panama or Mexico or wherever.
Wow! Too much! I feel like I'm in Panama or Mexico or wherever now.
Yeah. I know what you mean. Wherever's where it's at.
And that's where we are. Where it's at.
Yeah.
They start laughing idiotically again.
INT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT
Lorraine proceeds to sing --If I Had a Hammer|| in a pretty and forceful way.

(singing) If I had a hammer I'd hammer in the mornin' I'd hammer in the evenin' All over this land I'd hammer out danger I'd hammer out warnin' I'd hammer out love between My brothers and my sisters All over this land If I had a bell I'd ring it in the mornin' I'd ring it in the evenin' All over this land I'd ring out danger I'd ring out warnin' I'd ring out love between My brothers and my sisters All over this land If I had a song -
EXT. THE PURPLE ONION/ALLEY - NIGHT
Terry is holding forth to a stoned and completely rapt Phil.

Elvis and Chuck Berry and Little Richard and Jerry Lee Lewis all had their careers taken from them, man. Where'd they all go? What happened to rock & roll? They were stopped, drafted, arrested, persecuted. Their careers were stopped. Why? It's because there's a conspiracy to stop rock & roll, and I'm telling you it's the same guys that killed Kennedy. Phil has no idea what Terry's talking about.
Killed Kennedy? Lee Harvey Oswald killed Kennedy.
(lights up) Ah ha! That's what they'd like you to believe.
Then Jack Ruby killed Oswald.
Pretty convenient, huh? Get rid of the patsy.
But I saw it on TV.
Yeah? I see --Howdy Doody|| on TV but that don't make it true.
Phil looks all around, freaking out.
INT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT
Lorraine finishes the song with a flurry.
Well, I have a hammer
And I have a bell And I have a song to sing All over this land It's the hammer of justice It's the bell of free-e-dom It's a song about love between My brother and my sisters All over this land
Lorraine receives a big round of applause. She glances at the two empty seats and furrows her brow. The audience hollers for --More!||

All right. Now we're all gonna sing a song together, because we all have to stick together if we're gonna make things better. And we can all start by being here tomorrow night at eight to help put together the defense fund for the Springfield Five. OK? Now, instead of singing --the|| union, let's all sing --our|| union, OK? And if you don't know the verses, just join in for the chorus.
Lorraine begins to play the melody to --The Battle Hymn of the Republic|| which becomes --Solidarity Forever.|| The audience hesitantly joins in at first, but gets stronger with each chorus.

(singing) Solidarity forever Solidarity forever Solidarity forever For our union makes us strong. Through the worker's blood shall run There can be no power greater Anywhere beneath the sun Yet what force on Earth is weaker Than the feeble strength of one But our union makes us strong Solidarity forever

For our union makes us strong. It is we who plowed the prairies Built the cities where they trade Dug the mines and built the workshops Endless miles of railroad laid Now we stand outcast and starving _Mid the wonders we have made But our union makes us strong

Solidarity forever Solidarity forever Solidarity forever For our union makes us strong. They have taken untold millions That they never toiled to earn But without our brain and muscle Not a single wheel will turn We can break their haughty power Gain our freedom when we learn That our union makes us strong. Solidarity forever Solidarity forever For our union makes us strong. In our hands is placed the power Greater than their hoarded gold Greater than the might of atoms Magnified a thousand fold We can bring to birth a new world From the ashes of the old For our union makes us strong. (Lorraine call out) Now, come on! Everybody! (everybody joins in) Solidarity forever Solidarity forever For our union makes us strong.

There is the biggest round of applause of the evening. Everybody laughs and cheers. Lorraine returns to her table to the smiles of her friends, then seats herself between the two empty seats and frowns. Debbie is drunk and gives Lorraine a fresh beer and another cigarette, which Lorraine gratefully takes. Alvin keeps one hand on his wife's belly while drinking and smoking with the other.
(drunk) Good job, Lorraine. Solidarity forever!
(drunk) That was great, Lorraine, just great. (points at Debbie's belly) This kid is gonna grow up in a better world than what we got. What we got was shit. Old and wornout and busted.
Lorraine looks all around.
Have you seen Terry or Phil?
Alvin and Debbie look around.
No.
Lorraine stands.
I'll be back in a sec.
Lorraine leaves. Debbie drunkenly turns back to Alvin.
And that's why you've got to get a better job.
Oh, Jesus Christ! I don't give a shit about a better job, can't you get that through your thick skull?
I get it, but I'm not buyin' it.
Phil and Terry return and they're toasted and giggling. Terry gives Moustapha the thumbs-up.
I'll go get some more beer.
Good thinking.
Phil sits down beside Moustapha.
So, uh . . . Moustachio--
--Moustapha.
Exactly. So, what's it like being a Negro?
(laughs) Boy, you are stoned.
(looks around) Yeah? I guess so. Everything has sort of an echo.
--What's it like being a Negro?|| Shit! What's it like being a silly little stoned white boy?
(grins foolishly) Not bad. Pretty good, actually.

But that wears off. Bein' black don't. Everywhere you go, all the time, you still a Negro, and you're generally outnumbered, unless you're in the ghetto. All the white folk are friendly here tonight, but that don't mean they won't be back out lynchin' niggers
(this is heavy)
(sarcastic) Wow! Golly gee-whiz! (points at Phil) I was down in Birmingham last year for Dr. King's big march, the one where he got arrested. You hear about that?
Uh . . . maybe.

(shakes his head sadly) Shit! You don't know anything, do you? Your head's as empty as a bucket, that's why you hear an echo. What're you doin' here, anyway? You ain't no folkie. You ain't committed to no causes.
Phil points at Lorraine who is talking to a guy at another table.
She invited me.

(laughs) Yeah, the great recruiter, Sweet Lorraine. (gets serious) Y'know, man, we all stand for somethin', even if we don't know what it is. You can either stand for something on purpose or by mistake, but either way you still do. Think about it. Phil does think about it. When he looks up Moustapha has left and he's the only one

left sitting at the table. He immediately gets paranoid, thinking that everyone must be looking at him. He can't figure out what to do with his hands. Phil starts biting his nails. He stops himself and lights a cigarette from a pack on the table. Lorraine sits down beside Phil.
You missed my songs.
You sang more than one?
Two. They went over very well.
Well that's cool.
She looks Phil in the eyes and shakes her head in disapproval.
(whispers) You and Terry smoked pot?
(stutters) Um . . . That is . . . We, uh . . .
(shocked) That's so irresponsible.
What'dya mean?
Phil, you're about to go on.
Phil waves his hand, smiling.
No, no, no. There's still gotta be six or seven people ahead of me. He said they probably wouldn't get to me anyway.

Phil points at the blackboard and there are still quite a few people ahead of him, except the M.C. suddenly picks up the eraser and erases all the names above Phil's name. Now Phil is next. Phil's mouth drops open in panic.
Due to several last minute cancellations, may I introduce one more good friend of Lorraine's,

Phil Butler! Phil gets a squeeze on the arm from Lorraine and a thumbs-up from Terry, Moustapha, Alvin and Debbie. Phil makes his way onstage, which is a very long walk. He sits down on the stool, strums his guitar, coughs, squints up at the bright lights glaring in his eyes. PHIL That's Buckley, not Butler. The audience could really give a shit less. And sadly, Phil hasn't got the slightest clue what to play. This is the worst moment of his entire life. Phil suddenly has super-sonic hearing and sight - he can hear people breathing, puffing on cigarettes, moving their chairs, all looking at him expectantly. Phil moves closer to the lower, guitar microphone and gets feedback. Phil suddenly shoves the microphone into the hole in the guitar and proceeds to do the Elvis-style version of --If I Had a Hammer.||
If I had a h-h-h-hammer I'd h-h-h-hammer in the mornin' I'd h-h-h-hammer in the evenin' All over this crazy old land, oh yeah!
There is a big solid moment when the audience has absolutely no idea what they are watching. They look befuddled. Phil doesn't care. In for a penny in for a pound. He goes for it . . .
I'd-a hammer out danger I'd-a hammer out warnin' I'd-a hammer out-a love between My brothers and my sisters All over this land
Phil does the Chuck Berry duckwalk. The audience is sufficiently drunk so that they begin to clap along, hooting and hollering. Phil grabs two beer bottles and clanks them together.

If I had a bell I'd ring-a-ding-ding it in the mornin' I'd ring-a-ding-ding it in the evenin' All over this land I'd ring-a-ding-ding out danger I'd ring-a-ding-ding out warnin' I'd ring-a-ding-ding out love between My brothers and my sisters All over this land If I had a song You can just bet I'd sing it in the mornin' And I'd sing it in the evenin' All over this nutty old land I'd sing out danger I'd sing out warnin' I'd sing out love between My brothers and my sisters All over this land (gives a big pelvis shake) Ha!
There is a huge round of applause for Phil. He takes a bow, happily surprised at the turn of events. The M.C. comes on.

That's Phil Buckley, not Butler. Sorry about that, buddy. Very funny routine. You ought to be on Ed Sullivan. Anyway, thanks for coming to the Purple Onion's weekly Hootenany. And don't forget about the defense fund meeting for the Springfield Five tomorrow. And thanks for coming. Last call.
Many people stand up to leave. Lorraine looks at Phil in astonishment as he gulps down a beer. Terry is grinning and slaps Phil on the back.
This guy is a wild man.
I can't decide whether that was inspired or just pure nonsense.
Does it matter?
I don't know.
They all stand to leave. As everyone files out we can see numerous yellow flyers strewn hither, thither and yon. Lorraine sighs, picking some of them back up as she exits.
EXT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT
In front of the club Phil and Terry are watching Lorraine say goodbye to everyone and give out yellow flyers.
Remember the meeting is here tomorrow at
Many people say that they'll be there. Finally, the only people left are Lorraine, Phil and Terry. Lorraine confronts Phil and Terry.
I can't believe you missed my songs.
(abashed) Sorry.
Lorraine steps up to Phil's side and takes his arm.
Goodnight Terry.
Yeah, goodnight Terry. Nice meeting you.
Terry sees what's what.
Yeah, you too. _Night, Lorraine.
Terry leaves. Lorraine and Phil look at each other. The neon lights on the front of the building go out, plunging Lorraine and Phil into darkness.
You hurt my feelings.
Sorry. Did you like my song?
Honestly, no.
(hurt) You didn't?
You faked it.
But they liked it.
(confused) Hmmm . . .
Phil takes advantage of this moment and kisses Lorraine. They stand looking at each other.
I'm not really sure I like you.
(grins)
Me neither.
He kisses her again. Just then the M.C. steps out of the door. Phil and Lorraine's lips separate. The M.C. sees them lurking there.
Is that you, Lorraine?
Yeah, it is.
Still recruiting for the cause?
Up yours!
Goodnight
He leaves. Lorraine and Phil kiss again, longer. Lorraine steps away from him.
See you tomorrow?
Absolutely.
Lorraine walks away and Phil watches her go. Finally, Phil walks off in the other direction.
A title reads: --Sunday, February 9, 1964|| EXT. PHIL'S HOUSE - DAY
The morning sun shines over the top of Phil's house. Phil's father pushes a lawnmower out of the garage, pulls the rope and starts the noisy engine.
INT. PHIL'S BEDROOM - DAY

Phil lies in bed asleep. The sound of the lawnmower engine slowly wakes him up. Phil glances at the clock and sees that it's after 10:00 A.M. He's never slept this long in his life. He looks down at the pillow and sees a three-inch circle of drool.
(to himself) I feel like I got hit on the head with a hammer.
Phil glances over at his dresser where three folded-up yellow flyers repose. Phil scratches his head and a wistful smiles crosses his face as he recalls last night.
(to himself) Sweet Lorraine. (smiles) I knocked _em dead. They loved me.
INT. PHIL'S HOUSE/HALLWAY OUTSIDE BATHROOM - DAY
We can see the closed door of the bathroom and hear the water running in the shower. We can also hear Phil singing in the shower.

(O.S./singing) If I had a h-h-hammer I'd hammer a lot Then I'd h-h-hammer some more Then I'd hammer on the door I'd h-h-hammer all the nails Stickin' out of the log Then I'd h-h-hammer all the ants And I'd hammer all the dogs
Phil's mother and his brother meet in the hallway, stop and listen to Phil for a moment.
What's with him?
(shrugs) I guess he had a good time last night.
Holy Toledo, I sure hope he doesn't turn into
(sighs) It might be good for him. He's seemed awfully aimless lately. And those folk people do seem to care about things.
(skeptical) Yeah, I guess. But Ma . . .
Yes?
(confused) Do people just change like that?
(shrugs) Sometimes, I suppose.
His mother walks away. Dan considers her words for a second, then he too walks away. Phil keeps right on singing in the shower.
(O.S./singing) I'd h-h-hammer all the bugs Then I'd h-h-hammer all the bats Then I'd h-h-hammer all the cats Then I'd h-h-hammer all the gnats . . .
INT. PHIL'S BEDROOM - DAY
Phil stands in front of his mirror with wet hair, his guitar in hand, posing. He does his poor Ed Sullivan imitation.
We have a really big shew. For all of you folk fans that like Bob Dylan and Peter, Paul & Mary, I now give you the new voice of the nation, Phil
The crowds cheer wildly. Phil acts humble as he raises his guitar.
Thank you, thank you.
EXT. PHIL'S HOUSE - DAY
Phil's father is raking up the cut grass on the front lawn. Phil comes outside.
Here, let me help.
Excuse me?
Let me help.
(amazed) Huh. I'm not sure I've ever heard you say anything like that before.
(seriously) Look, dad, if we're all gonna get by in this world then I guess we'd better pitch in and help each other, right?
Right.
He hands Phil the rake. Phil shrugs, takes it and begins to rake. His father grabs the piles of cut grass and puts them in a trash can.
So, how was your date last night?
Good. Real good.
And you got up and sang?
Yep. And they liked me, too.
Well, I'll be damned. I'm really glad to hear it, Phil. So you're going to become a musician now?
Dad, I am a musician.
(shrugs) OK. All right. You sure got your money's worth out of those two weeks of guitar lessons.
(defensive) It was three weeks, OK? And I'd've kept it up, but my fingers hurt really bad, all right?
His father throws his hands up in capitulation.
All right, all right.
INT. PHIL'S HOUSE/KITCHEN - DAY
Phil's mother makes lunch while Phil lectures her.
Is it right for a woman to make less money doing the same job as a man?
Of course not.
That's right. And is it right for Negroes to have to sit at the back of the bus?
Well, no.
That's right, no. It's time to stand up and let our voices be heard.
His mother scoops gobs of mayonnaise into the tuna fish and nods in agreement.
So you sang a song last night?
Yeah.
How'd it go?
(casually) Great. They loved me.
(surprised) Really? That's very nice.
Phil walks away.
Oh yeah, they're a good crowd.
EXT. PHIL'S HOUSE/BACKYARD - DAY
Phil and Dan play catch with mitts and a hardball in the backyard.
So then the same guys that stopped rock & roll killed Kennedy.
(fascinated) Really? How?
It's very complicated and you probably won't
understand, but it's what's called a --conspiracy,|| which means that a bunch of people were involved.
Yeah? How many?
I don't know, but a bunch. So then, after they stopped rock & roll by sending Elvis away to the army, they decided they had to kill Kennedy to keep him quiet.
Really? That's creepy. What else did they do?
They set Lee Harvey Oswald up as a patsy, then
had Jack Ruby shoot him to shut him up. Dan is horrified.
Do mom and dad know this?
(shrugs helplessly) Probably not. They accept anything they're told. Just like robots.
EXT. THE PURPLE ONION - DAY

Max, the beatnik, loiters around in front of the Purple Onion smoking a cigarette. Lorraine comes driving up in her parents' enormous green Cadillac, every square inch of space jammed with wooden folding chairs. The trunk bulges open and is tied down with twine.
(shakes his head) Wow! What a Jew canoe.
That's not nice.
Lorraine, time to get a sense of humor. I was kidding.
Disparaging remarks about religion aren't funny.
What's wrong with you?
Where were you last night?
I hung out with some buddies, why?
You said you were coming to the Purple Onion?
I didn't make it.
Then why'd you say you were coming?
Lorraine, I don't owe you anything. I'm here unloading chairs out of a sense of commitment, not out of guilt.
I just don't like it when people say things and don't do them, that's all.
Yeah. Well get used to it.

Phil is getting dressed and rehearsing in front of the mirror.

(to himself) And I say, the Springfield Five must be freed or none of us is safe. If five Springfield youths can be put in jail for absolutely no good reason, then I say, who's next? (turns and yells) Mom!
(O.S.) Yes.
Have I got any clean socks?
(O.S.) In the laundry room.
Thanks. (continues) So I say, free the Springfield Five, that's what I say. And damnit, I mean what I say.

Phil steps into the living room once again dressed up and ready to go out, guitar case in hand. He finds his family in the exact same positions he left them last night, seated at TV tables watching TV. Dan looks at Phil and shakes his head sadly.
You're not going back to the Purple Onion again, are you?

(total disdain)
Nothin'. Only Ed Sullivan is about to go on now and The Beatles are gonna be on show tonight, that's all.
Phil is stricken.
The Beatles. Uh! I have to see them!
So sit down.
But I told this girl I'd go to her stupid meeting. She gave me three flyers.
He pulls out the three yellow flyers. Dan points at him and laughs.
(grins fiendishly) Ha ha!
Phil is paralyzed.
Now Phil, you told this girl you'd attend the meeting.
That's right, Phil. And what about the rights of the Negroes and women?
Dan looks right at Phil and speaks flatly.
The Beatles, Phil. --She Loves You, Yeah, Yeah, Yeah,|| --I Wanna Hold Your Hand,|| --Twist & Shout.||
(immobilized) Uh . . .
INT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT
Lorraine comes rushing into the Purple Onion in a fluster, her arms loaded with grocery bags, as well as her guitar. The M.C. is waiting for her impatiently.
For God's sake, Lorraine, I'm gonna miss Ed Sullivan.
Oh, for goodness' sake, Ed Sullivan is just an old gossip columnist.
(befuddled) There are times, Lorraine, when I don't understand you at all.

He gives her the key to the club, then rushes out. Lorraine takes off her coat and begins to hurriedly set things up for the meeting. The 50 folding chairs still need unfolding. She tacks a hand-made poster up on the wall that reads, --Free The
Springfield Five!|| INT. PHIL'S HOUSE/LIVING ROOM - NIGHT

Phil still stands there looking at his family and his family sits there looking back at him. The opening title music for --The Ed Sullivan Show|| begins and Phil's guitar case hits the floor. Phil seats himself on the couch beside his mother, who is knitting. Blue light flickers on their faces as we hear The Ed Sullivan Show begin.

Every single house on the block is emitting blue illumination and is tuned to Ed Sullivan. ED SULLIVAN

Lorraine is just finishing setting up. She has all 50 folding chairs unfolded and arranged in rows, piles of flyers lined up on a table, several jugs of cider and paper cups. She gives everything a final inspection, straightening this, turning that. She sighs, looking around expectantly, glancing down at her watch.
INT. PHIL'S HOUSE/LIVING ROOM - NIGHT
Phil and his family stare at the TV. A piece of classical music ends.
(O.S.) Let's give a nice warm hand to the St. Petersburg ballet. (polite applause is heard) I would now like to read a telegram that I received

INT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT

--Lorraine sits looking down at the yellow flyer announcing the meeting. She crumples it up and throws it away. We then see that she is sitting among 49 empty folding chairs. Lorraine picks up her guitar and exits. A second later all the lights go off.
EXT. THE PURPLE ONION - NIGHT
Lorraine steps out of the Purple Onion and locks the door. Just then Phil comes running up, guitar case in hand. He sees Lorraine and smiles.
Hi. Am I late?
(laughs) Are you late?
Am I?
(flatly) You missed it. It's over.
(casually) Whoops. Sorry about that. Did it go all right?
Look around. You see anyone else?
(looks around) Uh . . . no. Break up early?
(flatly) Yeah, it broke up early.
So, you wanna get a cup of coffee?
Lorraine laughs sardonically.
You don't give a shit at all, do you?
About what?
About anything.
(offended) Hey! I care about a lotta stuff.
Like what?
Well, like music.
Ha! You can't even play the Goddamn guitar. I mean, for Christ sake, how are you ever going to be a musician?
I told ya, it's possible.
So is getting hit by a meteor. Y'know, you're what the world is coming to, Phil, and I don't like it!
Apathy and inability. It's a really bad combination.
What's gotten into you? I thought we had a really good time last night.
(pointing) You didn't show up to my Goddamn meeting!
I said I was sorry.

(uncertain) What?
Drop dead!
Lorraine turns and walks away.

Italy. (she waves)
Lorraine stomps away, disappearing into the night. Phil stands there looking dazed.
(to himself) Huh.
Just then Terry comes running up from the other direction, also carrying a guitar case.
Did I miss the meeting?
We both did.

Oh yeah, they were boss!
I'm never cuttin' my hair again.
Boy oh boy, those girls were really screaming, huh?
Man, they were crying. I've never seen anything like it.
Me neither.
I like John.
Really. I like George. He sure can play that guitar. They both looked geeked up.

Terry waves his hand disdainfully.
The Springfield Five. I mean, who gives a crap? Not me.
(chuckles) Not me, either.
Wanna come over my place? We could smoke some more reefer, y'know, maybe jam a little bit? What'dya say?
Really? Cool.
